segments.s706-g97: همه اسپ را تنگها برکشید is a past-tense clause with an omitted subject and the verb برکشید at the end. همه modifies اسپ (’all the horses’) and را marks اسپ as the definite/direct object. تنگها functions adverbially/qualificationally (poetic plural/adverb) meaning ‘tightly’ or ‘close’ and comes before the verb to qualify the action of drawing/tightening.
segments.s706-g98: همه گرد این بیشه لشکر کشید is another past-tense clause with the verb کشید at the end. همه + گرد این بیشه together mean ‘all around this grove’; گرد is the postposition/adverb ‘around’. لشکر is the object/subject of کشید depending on reading (here ‘they drew/moved the host’). The verb کشید (here ‘to draw up/move’) is poetic and not exactly one of the supplied senses, so its sense is marked as not applicable.
segments.s706-g99: سواران سبک برکشیدند تنگ has an explicit plural subject سواران سبک (‘light horsemen’ or ‘light cavalry’ — noun + adjectival modifier) and a plural past verb برکشیدند. تنگ is adverbial here (‘tightly’). Word order is subject — (adj) — verb, with the adverb following the verb phrase in this line for emphasis.
segments.s706-g100: گرفتند شمشیر هندی به چنگ places the verb گرفتند (‘they seized/took’) before its object شمشیر هندی (‘Indian sword’). به چنگ is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘into/into the hand’ (here ‘in hand’ / ‘gripped’). The clause describes the action performed by the same plural subject.
segments.s706-g101: همه نیستان آتش اندر زدند is a past-tense clause where همه modifies نیستان (‘the entire reed-bed’). اندر is the locative/prepositional element ‘in/within’, and زدند is the past plural of زدن ‘to strike’ used causatively here: ‘they struck (i.e., set) fire in the reed-bed’ = ‘they set the reed-bed ablaze.’
segments.s706-g102: سپه را یکایک بهم بر زدند has سپه را as the marked object/target (‘the army’). یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one / each in turn’. بهم (here) conveys ‘into confusion/together’ and بر زدند is a compound usage of زدن with the prefix/particle بر meaning ‘to strike/throw (into a state)’; overall: ‘they struck/attacked the army, one by one, throwing it into disorder.’
segments.s706-g103: نیستان سراسر شد افروخته is a copular/predicative construction: نیستان (subject) + سراسر شد (‘became throughout’ / ‘was wholly’) + افروخته (past participle used as predicate adjective ‘ablaze/kindled’). The order is noun — adverbial/completive — auxiliary — adjective, typical in poetic past narration.
segments.s706-g104: یکی کشته و دیگری سوخته is a terse nominal-phrase listing results: یکی (‘one [person]’) + کشته (past participle ‘killed/slain’), و (‘and’), دیگری (‘the other’) + سوخته (‘burned’). Each participle describes the fate of different individuals in the scene.
segments.s706-g81: به + اسم + گفتند is the dative construction: به بهرام گفتند = 'they said to Bahram.' گفتند is past‑tense, 3rd person plural 'they said.' انوشه بدی is presented as a vocative or name phrase addressed by the speakers; here the two words function together as the form of address rather than a syntactic predicate.
segments.s706-g82: ز راه نیستان چرا آمدی is a short interrogative clause. ز + راه = 'from/by the road/path'; نیستان 'reed‑bed' supplies the specific route. چرا آمدی is a direct question 'why did you come?' with آمدی in simple past (2nd person singular). Word order is locative phrase then the question.
segments.s706-g83: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). سپاهست is a fused form meaning 'it is an army/there is a host' (poetic compound). پیش اندرون = 'before the inner part/inner court' (پیش = 'in front of/before', اندرون = 'inside/inner quarters'). بی‌مر is an archaic/obscure adjective modifying سپاهست; its exact meaning is uncertain in this text.
segments.s706-g84: همه جنگ را has the object/topic marker را attached to جنگ ('the whole battle/ all fighters'); دست شسته به خون is a participial/compound phrase 'hands washed in blood' where شسته (washed) describes دست. The preposition-like به + noun (here خون) indicates instrument/medium ('with/by blood' — i.e. bloody hands). Adjectival phrase follows the object it qualifies as is common in Persian poetic order.
segments.s706-g85: چنین 'thus' is an adverb setting the manner; گفت is past 'said' with بهرام as the speaker. کایدر is an archaic/dialectal particle here introducing the noun سوار (rider/horseman); it functions like a restrictive or interrogative introducer in older Persian and is not productive in modern usage.
segments.s706-g86: نباشد is a negative jussive/optative sense 'let there not be' or 'should not be' (here third‑person negative jussive). جز از = 'except from/of'; لشکر شهریار = 'the king's army/host' (لشکر + genitive/possessive relation شهریار). The clause sets an exclusion: no rider except one belonging to the king's force.
segments.s706-g87: فرود آمدند is past plural 'they descended/ alighted.' اندر ان (here written اندران) = 'in/among there' (locative adverb) and نیستان 'reed‑bed' names the place. The verb comes before the locative phrase, a frequent order in Persian narrative poetry.
segments.s706-g88: همه جنگ را is 'the whole battle/ all the fighters' marked by را. تنگ بسته is a compound adjective/participial phrase 'narrow‑closed' = 'closely confined/hemmed in' where تنگ 'narrow' modifies بسته 'closed/bound.' میان functions as a locative adverb 'between/in the midst.' Together they describe that the fighting was tightly confined between them.
segments.s706-g57: This is a compact clause with the dative/prepositional phrase 'بدو' (to her/him) before the verb — a common classical word order. 'گفت' is the reporting verb ‘said’; 'بهرام' is the speaker's name in apposition; 'کای' is the vocative particle 'O'; 'پاک' is an epithet meaning 'chaste/pure' modifying 'زن' (woman). In other words: 'He said to her: “O chaste woman...”'.
segments.s706-g58: 'مرا اندرین داستانی بزن' is an imperative request directed at the addressee. 'مرا' is the first‑person object clitic (here split in the gloss as 'م' + 'را') marking 'me'; 'اندرین داستانی' = 'in/this tale' (اندرین = in this; داستانی = tale/story); 'بزن' is an imperative of زدن used idiomatically as 'deliver/say/tell' (literally 'strike' but here 'go ahead — tell it').
segments.s706-g59: This subordinate clause begins with causal 'که' (here 'for/because'). 'این' = 'this'; 'از خرد بود' = literally 'was from wisdom' meaning 'was wise/prudent'; word order places the verb 'بود' after its complement (typical of classical Persian). 'بهرام را' uses the enclitic 'را' to mark the person affected/concerned (roughly 'on Bahram's part' or 'to Bahram').
segments.s706-g60: 'وگر' = 'and if / but if' introduces a counterfactual/conditional. 'برگزید' is the past/perfect 'chose'; 'از هوا کام را' is a genitive-like phrase 'desire (کام) from passion/whim (هوا)' with 'را' marking the (grammatical) object. Overall: 'and if he had chosen desire from (mere) passion...' (the conditional is left open).
segments.s706-g61: 'بدو پیرزن گفت' = 'the old woman said to him' with 'بدو' (to him) preceding the subject and verb, a normal classical ordering. 'کای' is the vocative 'O'; 'شهره' here is adjectival 'famed/renowned' modifying 'مرد' (man/hero). So: 'the old woman said to him, “O renowned man...”'.
segments.s706-g62: An interrogative clause: 'چرا' = 'why'; 'دیو' = 'demon/fiend' (subject); 'چشم تو را' = 'your eye' with 'را' marking the (definite) object of 'تیره کرد' (made dark); 'تیره کرد' = 'darkened/obscured' (literally 'made dark'). Word order is SOV (subject دیو — object چشم تو را — verb تیره کرد).
segments.s706-g63: 'ندانی' is the negative present second person singular of 'دانستن' = 'you do not know' (classical form). It is followed by the complementizer 'که' introducing the content clause. 'بهرام پور گشسپ' is a name phrase 'Bahram, son of Gushasp' (پور = 'son of' attached to the father's name). This whole segment is 'Don't you know that Bahram, son of Gushasp...'.
segments.s706-g64: This clause names another person and an action. 'چوبا پور هرمز' should be read as 'Chubā, son of Hormoz' (پور = 'son of', هرمز a personal name). 'بر انگیزد' is a poetic/present-tense form of 'انگیختن/انگیزیدن' meaning 'stirs/rouses/sets in motion' (here 3rd person), and 'اسپ' = 'steed/horse' is the object. The result: '(... ) Chubā son of Hormoz roused a steed.' (The classical word order places the verb after the agent phrase.)
segments.s706-g41: 'بریدم' is a past first-person verb ('I cut') and comes before its object; 'کدو را' is the direct object with the object-marker 'را'. The particle 'که' introduces a clause modifying the gourd (here = 'whose/for which'), and 'نوبد' is an archaic spelling of 'نو بود' ('was new'). 'سرش' = 'سر + ش' is 'its top/head', so the clause says something like 'whose top was new'.
segments.s706-g42: 'یکی' = 'one/a single' introduces the item; 'جام کردم' uses 'کردم' as a light verb with 'جام' to mean 'I made/turned into a cup' (literal: 'I did/made a cup'). 'نهادم' is another past tense verb 'I placed/put', and 'برش' here functions as 'بر + ش' ('on it' / 'upon it') referring back to the gourd — the two verbs describe making the cup and placing it on/into the gourd. The order is typical: object/measure + light-verb, then verb of placing.
segments.s706-g43: The phrase begins with 'بدو' = 'to her' (dative clitic + demonstrative), then 'گفت' (past of 'to say') introduces direct speech; 'بهرام' names the speaker. 'چون می‌بود' is an interrogative/optative construction: 'چون' = 'how' and 'می‌بود' is the imperfect/modal past 'would be/was' producing 'how would it be' or 'how could it be'.
segments.s706-g44: 'ازان' = 'from that/of those' points back to the previously mentioned cups; 'خوبتر' is the comparative 'better'; 'جامها' is the plural 'cups'; 'کی بود' is a rhetorical question 'who/which was (better)?' — the clause asks rhetorically which cups could be better than those.
segments.s706-g45: زن پیر is noun + adjective with the adjective after the noun (literally “woman old” = “old woman”). رفت و بیاورد is a sequence of two simple-past verbs joined by و, describing successive actions (“went and brought”); بیاورد here is the 3rd-person singular past (literary narrative form) meaning “brought.” جام is the direct object (a cup) associated with بیاورد; in poetry the object may follow the verb or be placed before it for rhythm.
segments.s706-g46: ازان is the fused older form of از آن (“from that”) and here begins a prepositional phrase. The clause بهرام شد شادکام uses شد (past of شدن) as the copular verb “became” and شادکام is a predicate adjective meaning “joyful/fortunate”; word order is prepositional phrase + subject + verb + predicate (“From that cup Bahram became joyful”).
segments.s706-g47: یکی at the sentence start is an indefinite “one/someone/a certain (person).” جام پر is a noun + modifier meaning “a full cup” (پر = full of). بر کفش is a locative phrase “on the shoe” (بر = on; کفش = shoe). برنهاد is the literary past of نهادن, 3rd-person singular “placed/put (upon),” so the whole clause reads “Someone placed a full cup on the shoe.”
segments.s706-g48: بدان here functions as an adverbial/relative particle meaning “by that / with that” (an older fused form roughly = به آن) and introduces the purpose/result clause. تا is the purpose marker “so that / in order that,” and شود (from شدن) is used optatively/subjunctively “may become / would become.” پیرزن نیز شاد is the result clause “the old woman also [become] happy” (نیز = also; شاد = happy). Together: “by that, so that the old woman too would become happy.”
segments.s706-g121: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when' and governs the past verb that follows; بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard'. نستود is an archaic/past form of ستودن ('he praised') that follows the temporal clause; روی زمین is a prepositional/locative phrase 'on the ground' (روی + زمین) and is the place associated with the following action(s).
segments.s706-g122: ببوسید is 3rd-person past 'he kissed' (object 'the ground' is implied from the previous phrase). و links actions. بسیار modifies کرد and means 'much'/'repeatedly'; کرد is the light verb 'did' used with the noun آفرین to mean 'uttered praise'; آفرین is the noun/exclamation 'bravo' — together 'بسیار کرد آفرین' = 'he said “afarin” many times' or 'he praised profusely'.
segments.s706-g123: وزان is a fused connective 'and/from that' pointing back to the previous place or event; بیشه 'grove' is the locative source; the clause 'بهرام شد تابری' uses شد as a linking/predicative verb 'became' — 'Bahram became (known as) Tabari' (تابری = 'of Tabar/Tabaristan' or an epithet).
segments.s706-g124: ابا here functions archaically like با 'with' (classical/poetic spelling) introducing accompaniment: 'with him'. او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'him'. دلیران is the plural substantive 'the brave ones'. فرخنده is an adjective 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying دلیران. پی is an adjectival/participial element 'following'/'in his train' (i.e. they followed him).
segments.s706-g125: ببود is an archaic past-form meaning 'he/it was' (literary). و simply links clauses. برآسود is a past/predicative compound 'was relieved/at rest' (برـ + آسودن). ز آنجا 'from there' is the source phrase; برفت is the older past of رفتن 'he departed/went away' (here the verb is the action of leaving).
segments.s706-g126: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near to'; خاقان is the title 'khaghan' (ruler). خرامید is 3rd-person past of خرامیدن 'he strode/pranced proudly' describing the manner of approach. تفت at the end is obscure here (either a name or a textual corruption) and cannot be matched to the given lexical senses with confidence.
segments.s706-g73: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید is the grammatical subject 'the sun'; برچرخ is a locative/adverbial phrase 'upon the wheel/heavens'; بگشاد is a past tense verb (poetic prefix ب- + گشاد) meaning 'opened/revealed'; راز is the direct object 'secret'. Word order is slightly poetic (conjunction + subject + locative + verb + object).
segments.s706-g74: سپهدار جنگی is the subject phrase ('the commander, warlike/commander of war'), with جنگی an epithet; بزد is past 3rd-person singular 'he struck'; طبل is the object 'drum'; باز is an adverb here meaning 'again'. The clause therefore reads 'the war-commander struck the drum again.'
segments.s706-g75: بیاورد is a past verb 'he brought/assembled'; چندانک is an adverb 'to that extent / so much'; بودش is the enclitic past copula 'there was to him / he had' (poetic contracted form); سپاه is the noun object 'an army/host'. Together: 'he brought/assembled so many that he had an army.'
segments.s706-g76: گرانمایگان is the subject 'the illustrious/esteemed ones (nobles)'; برگرفتند is past plural 'they took up / set out (took)'; راه is the object 'the road / way' — a common idiom meaning 'they set out on the road.' Word order is subject + verb + object.
segments.s706-g77: بره is the subject 'a lamb'; بر + یکی + نیستان is a locative expression 'on a certain reed-bed' (بر = on; یکی = a certain/one; نیستان = reed-bed); بود is the past copula 'was'; نو is the predicate adjective 'new/fresh'. The order (subject — locative — verb — adjective) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s706-g78: بسی functions adverbially 'many / much'; اندرو is locative 'in it / therein' referring back to the reed-bed; مردم is 'people'; نی‌درو literally denotes those who reap or cut reeds (here functioning as a description/noun phrase 'reed-cutters'). The clause means 'many people there were reed-cutters.'
segments.s706-g79: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'; دیدند is past plural 'they saw'; بهرام را is the definite object with را marking emphasis/topic 'Bahram'. Normal order here is conjunction + adverbial + verb + object.
segments.s706-g80: چنان is a demonstrative 'such / so'; لشکرگشن is a compound meaning a hungry/famished army or voracious host (poetic epithet); و is the conjunction 'and'; خودکام is an adjective 'self-willed / autocratic'; را again is the object/topic marker applying to the whole noun phrase, so the phrase describes what they saw alongside Bahram.
segments.s706-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. خورشید روشن is a noun + adjective (literally 'sun bright') in poetic order meaning 'the bright sun'. بیاراست is a past tense verb 'adorned/arrayed' whose object is گاه ('hour/time'), so the whole phrase means 'when the bright sun arrayed the hour'.
segments.s706-g2: طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'out of' (here functioning locatively), followed by نزدیک شاه 'near the king' indicating the point from/at which the vanguard approached.
segments.s706-g3: به here functions as a locative/into 'in/within' before پرده سرای اندرون ('the curtained inner house/chamber'). کس ندید is a negative indefinite construction meaning 'no one saw' (کس = 'no one' here, ندید = past 'saw' used with the negative sense).
segments.s706-g4: همان is the demonstrative 'that very/that same' modifying خیمه 'tent'. بر پای is a locative phrase 'at the foot/base' (بر = 'on/upon', پای = 'foot/base'). بر بس is an emphatic/poetic phrasing (بس here functions adverbially 'merely/at all'), and ندید is the past 'did not see'—together conveying that that tent at its foot was also not seen.
segments.s706-g5: Here a sequence of actions is reported: طلایه (the vanguard) + بیامد (came) + بگفت (said/told). این is the demonstrative object 'this' (what was said), and به شاه marks the recipient with the preposition 'to' ('told this to the king').
segments.s706-g6: دل شاه is a possessive phrase 'the king's heart' (دل + شاه). شد تنگ is a light verb construction with شد 'became' and predicate تنگ 'constricted/afflicted' (an idiom meaning 'became anxious or distressed'). زان is a causal/relative particle 'because of/for that', linking to رزمخواه 'the war‑desirer / eager fighter' as the cause.
segments.s706-g7: گزین کرد is a compound past tense verb 'selected/chose' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made). زان is 'from that' or 'of those', pointing back to the previously mentioned fighters; here it governs the group from which the selection was made. جنگیان refers to 'fighters/warriors' (plural) as the source group.
segments.s706-g8: This segment lists the qualities of the selected troops. زره دار functions as 'armored' (literally 'having armor'), joined by و 'and' to additional descriptors; برگستوان (an archaic/compound term) and ور (an archaic particle) qualify the following سوار 'horseman/rider'—together describing heavily armed/mounted men. Several lemmas here are poetic/compound formations rather than the literal senses given, so some sense indices are unspecified.
segments.s706-g17: This is an appositional epithet naming and praising the hero: یلان (heroes) followed by two bodily-metaphor epithets سینه و گرد attached to the name ایزد گشسپ (Goshasp). Word order is poetic: nouns and epithets are juxtaposed rather than linked by a copula; the phrase as a whole identifies Goshasp as a foremost warrior. The conjunction و simply links the two epithetic nouns 'سینه' and 'گرد'.
segments.s706-g18: ز یک سوی لشکر همی‌راند اسپ is a verbal clause giving the action: ز یک سوی لشکر 'from one side/wing of the army' is an adverbial phrase of place (ز + سوی + لشکر), and همی‌راند اسپ is a present-imperfective narrative clause 'was riding/was driving a horse/steed'. The verb همی‌راند is third-person plural/singular-old-style continuous narration; here it belongs to the named hero (Goshasp) in the preceding segment.
segments.s706-g19: به بی‌راه لشکر همی‌راندند is a clause where به بی‌راه functions adverbially ('into/onto a wrong road' or 'astray'), لشکر is the object/affected group 'the host/army', and همی‌راندند is the past-continuous third-person plural verb 'they were driving/leading'. The construction is narrative (همی- prefix indicates ongoing action in classical/poetic Persian).
segments.s706-g20: سخنهای شاهان همی‌خواندند is a coordinated clause: سخنهای (the plural possessive form of سخن) 'the words/speeches', شاهان is the possessor 'of the kings', and همی‌خواندند is the imperfective past plural 'they were reciting/singing'. The genitive/attributive relationship is indicated by juxtaposition (senses: words of kings).
segments.s706-g21: پدید آمد از دور یک پاره ده is a simple narrative clause: پدید آمد (appeared) is the past tense verb, از دور is the locative 'from afar', and یک پاره ده is a poetic noun phrase 'a single torn/fragmentary village/hamlet' (پاره modifies ده). Word order places the verb before the locative and the noun phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s706-g22: کجا ده نبود از در مرد مه is a negative existential statement: نبود is the past negative 'there was not', کجا here functions as 'at which place' / 'there' (introducing the negation), از در literally 'at/from the door/gate', and مرد مه is a noun + epithet meaning 'a great/manly man' (مه used as 'great'). Together: 'there was no great man at the gate.'
segments.s706-g23: همی‌راند بهرام پیش اندرون is a clause where همی‌راند (imperfective past) 'was riding/driving' is the verb, بهرام is the subject, and پیش اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'before/toward the inner (quarters)' or 'in front of the inner court'. The order places the verb first (poetic narration) then subject and locative.
segments.s706-g24: پشیمان شده دل پر از درد و خون is a participial/adjectival phrase describing Bahram's state: پشیمان شده 'having become remorseful' (شده is a past participle/auxiliary), followed by a nominal predicate دل پر از درد و خون 'a heart full of pain and blood' where پر از means 'full of' and the coordination درد و خون lists the contents. The whole phrase modifies or comments on the subject in the previous segment.
segments.s706-g9: به marks the indirect object 'to' (he spoke to someone). نستود here is a proper name (Nastud), so it is the addressee. فرمود is a past verb 'said/commanded' used here as an order. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/command) clause 'that/so that'. برنشست is a past action 'mounted/sat up' and completes the commanded action.
segments.s706-g10: میان = 'in/among' (locative 'amidst'). یلی refers to the valiant/champions (adjective-noun use). تاختن is the verbal noun 'the charging/charge' (military gallop). را is the definite-object marker attaching to the verbal noun. ببست is a past verb 'shut/checked/closed' — here 'he stopped/checked the charge'.
segments.s706-g11: همی‌راند is the imperfective past 'was riding/was driving' (the prefix همی marks continuous action). نستود is the subject (proper name) and here indicates who is riding. دل پر ز درد is an adverbial/state phrase 'heart full of pain' (دل = heart, پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', درد = emotional/poetic 'pain/sorrow'), describing his inner state while riding.
segments.s706-g12: نبد is an archaic negative/existential past 'there was not/there was no'. مرد = 'man/hero' (here 'a man' in the comparative sense). بهرام is the proper name Bahram. روز نبرد = 'the day of battle' (روز = day, نبرد = battle), a temporal genitive phrase attached after the noun.
segments.s706-g13: همان نیز = 'that same/also' (emphatic 'that very one' + 'also'). بهرام is the proper name Bahram. با لشکرش = 'with his army' (با = with; لشکرش = army + possessive suffix 'his'). This phrase sets the subject/topic.
segments.s706-g14: نبود is past 'was not' (negative copula/existence). ایمن = 'safe/protected'. از marks 'from' (the source or danger). راه = 'road/way' (here 'on the roads'). وز is an archaic fused form (= و ز = 'and from') linking two sources and so functions as 'and (from)'; it is an old/contracted particle. کشورش = 'his country/realm' (کشور = country; ش = his). Together: 'was not safe from the road and (from) his country.'
segments.s706-g15: همی‌راند again is the continuous past 'was riding/driving'. بی‌راه here means 'off the road/astray; wrong/unwarranted' — i.e., riding away from the right way. دل پر ز بیم is the same state phrase 'his heart full of fear' (دل = heart; پر = full; ز = of/from; بیم = fear/anxiety), stating his emotional condition.
segments.s706-g16: همی‌برد is the imperfective past 'was carrying / carried away' (ongoing action). با خویشتن = 'with himself' (با = with; خویشتن = himself/self, emphatic object). زر و سیم = 'gold and silver' (direct objects); the order is typical 'noun و noun'. The whole clause: 'he was carrying gold and silver with him.'
segments.s706-g49: بدو گفت is a light phrase meaning 'he said to her' (بدو = 'to him/to her' as an indirect-object clitic/adverbial). کای is the vocative 'O'. مام is an address‑word 'mother' (here used as an honorific/term of address for an older woman). با فرهی is a postpositive descriptive phrase: با = 'with' and فرهی = 'learned/cultured', functioning like an epithet attached to the vocative.
segments.s706-g50: ز کار جهان is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'of/ concerning the affairs of the world' (ز = 'from/of', کار = 'affair/ business', جهان = 'world'). چیستت is چیست + enclitic ت (2nd‑person object/possessive) and functions roughly as 'what is to you' = 'what do you know'; آگهی is the noun 'information/knowledge' acting as the predicate/object tied to چیستت.
segments.s706-g51: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object). پیرزن is the subject 'the old woman'. گفت is the reported‑speech verb 'said' (past) introducing what follows. چندان سخن is a quantified noun phrase 'so much talk' (چندان = 'so much/that much', سخن = 'speech/talk').
segments.s706-g52: شنیدم is the first‑person past 'I heard' (reports the speaker's experience). کزان = 'from which / from that' (a relative/causal adverb linking back to 'چندان سخن'). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', so گشت مغزم کهن = 'my brain became old'; مغزم is مغز + enclitic م 'my brain', کهن is predicate adjective 'old/aged'.
segments.s706-g53: ز شهر آمد امروز is a temporal/location clause 'from the city came today' where the verb آمد precedes the subject (a common poetic/narrative order). بسیار کس = 'many people' (بسیار quantifies کس 'person/people'), functioning as the (postverbal) subject.
segments.s706-g54: همی ... گویند is a conventional reporting formula 'they say' (همی is a continuous/emphatic particle here). جنگ چوبینه is a noun phrase 'a wooden / cudgel fight' (چوبینه indicates 'wooden' or 'with clubs'). گویند is the reported‑speech verb 'they say' and بس at the end means 'only/merely' or 'and that's all'.
segments.s706-g55: که شد at the start introduces a circumstantial/temporal clause 'when/that it came to pass'. لشکر او 'his army' is the noun phrase (لشکر = army, او = his). به نزدیک شاه = 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه = toward/near the king).
segments.s706-g56: سپهبد is the subject/title 'the spahbed'. گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing' (describing his state or action). بشد is the past/resultive 'became/was' (here 'became fleeing' = 'took flight'), and بی‌سپاه is the resulting state 'without troops' (بی = without + سپاه = army/troops).
segments.s706-g65: بخندد is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person jussive/subjunctive (b- prefix + خندِد) meaning 'let him laugh' or 'he should laugh'. برو is an imperative 'go' placed after the verb for terse, imperative exhortation. هرک دارد خرد is a relative-like clause 'whoever has wisdom' (هرک = 'whoever'; دارد present tense 'has'; خرد = 'wisdom'), modifying the implied subject of بخندد/برو.
segments.s706-g66: کس here, in the negative clause that follows, means 'no one'. اورا is the third-person object pronoun 'him'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'among'/'of'), and گردنکشان 'neck‑throwers' refers to rebellious/proud people; نشمرد is the negated present/past form of شمارَد 'count/deem' meaning 'does not consider/does not reckon'. Together: 'no one will deem him one of the neck‑throwers.'
segments.s706-g67: بدو گفت is a common classical sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بد = 'to him' + گفت = 'said'). بهرام‌گر is the speaker's name, placed after the verb phrase in poetic order, and آرزوی is 'wish' (object or topic) introducing what follows.
segments.s706-g68: چنین کرد is an instruction 'do thus' or 'do it this way'. گو here functions as an introductory imperative/quotative 'say' or 'so: ' and introduces the command that follows. می‌خوران is the causative/present form 'make (someone) drink' or 'force to drink', در is the preposition 'in', and کدوی refers to a gourd (a hollowed squash used as a container). The clause gives the concrete action: 'make him drink in a gourd.'
segments.s706-g69: برین گونه is the fused poetic form of بر این گونه 'in this manner' (بر = on/in, ین = این 'this', گونه = 'manner'). غربیل بر نان جو is a noun phrase in inverted/poetic order: غربیل 'sieve' + بر 'on/upon' + نان جو 'barley‑bread' (bread made of barley). The preposition بر follows the noun in this construction, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s706-g70: همی‌دار is a present/habitual form meaning 'keep/continue to hold' (همی- marks ongoing action). در پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front (of it)'. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until', جو 'barley', and درو is the verb 'to reap/harvest' in infinitive/nominal sense; together: 'keep it in front until the barley is reaped.'
segments.s706-g71: بران is a connective adverb 'thereupon' / 'for that reason' (بر + ان). هم adds 'also' or 'too'. خورش is 'stew' or 'cooked dish' and is the thing associated with that night; یک شب آرام یافت is a verbal clause 'found one peaceful night' (یافت = 'found/obtained'), so the whole segment says they had/obtained one quiet night with the stew.
segments.s706-g72: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle 'still/indeed'. کام دل 'the heart's desire' (کام = desire; دل = heart) is the object of جست here; جست in this context means 'sought' (sense = 'seek'), and ناکام یافت is a compound: ناکام 'unsuccessful' + یافت 'found', literally 'found (himself) unsuccessful' — i.e. he sought his heart's desire but was unsuccessful.
segments.s706-g113: مکش is a prohibitive imperative 'do not pull/take' (classical Persian negative imperative). مر م(را) here represents the object 'me' in an older/poetic form (the pronoun and the object marker are split/emphatic in the line). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that', followed by دوان 'run/rush' (verbal idea: 'let me run') and پیش تو 'in front of you / up to you'. Word order is typical older Persian: verb (imperative) + object + purpose clause.
segments.s706-g114: بیایم is an optative/subjunctive 'may I come' or 'that I may come' (expressing the intended result). بوم here means 'dwelling / abode' rather than 'homeland'. زار is adjectival 'wretched / pitiable'. درویش is 'poor/dervish' (a humble/poverty-marked person) and تو is the possessive 'your' placed after the noun in the classical style.
segments.s706-g115: بدو is an indirect-object prepositional form 'to him / unto him'. گفت is the past 'said' (here marking reported speech) and بهرام is the subject 'Bahram' (word order 'to him said Bahram' is common in older Persian). من چون تو مرد is the content of the speech: 'I, like you, [am] a man' (چون = 'like/as').
segments.s706-g116: نخواهم is the first-person negative of 'خواستن' — 'I do not want / I will not wish'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that' and باشد is the subjunctive/present 'there be / exist'. به دشت نبرد literally 'battle in the plain' (به = 'in/to', دشت = 'plain', نبرد = 'battle'); together the clause means 'that there be fighting on the plain'.
segments.s706-g117: نبرم is the first-person negative/optative form of بردن here 'I will not take / I will not seize'. سرت را is the object 'your head' with the object marker را. که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/because', ننگ 'shame' and آیدم 'would come to me / befall me' (آیدم used idiomatically as 'befall me').
segments.s706-g118: که continues the subordinate/explanatory clause. چون = 'like/as', تو = 'you', سواری = 'rider/horseman' (descriptive noun), and به جنگ آیدم = 'I should come to the battle/war' (آیدم here expresses the idea 'would come / should come'). The whole clause gives the reason: shame would fall on me that I, like you, came to fight as a mounted man.
segments.s706-g119: چو is conditional 'when/if'. یابی is second-person present/subjunctive 'you find / you obtain'. رهایی is the noun 'release / freedom' and ز دستم literally 'from my hand' = 'from my power / control'. بپوی is an imperative 'seek! / pursue!' directed at the addressee (go after it).
segments.s706-g120: ز من 'from/about me' (z + pronoun used to mark source or concerning). هرچ = 'whatever', دیدی is second-person past 'you saw' (reporting what the listener has seen), به خسرو = 'to Khosrow' (recipient), and بگوی is imperative 'tell' (order: report whatever you have seen to Khosrow).
segments.s706-g105: The phrase has the object marked with را (چونستود را) followed by the verb دید and the subject بهرام گرد placed after the verb for emphasis; دید is simple past 3rd-person 'he saw'. Here 'چونستود' functions as a definite object (likely a proper name or person) and را confirms it as the object. 'بهرام گرد' is the subject (Bahrām Gōrd — a compound name) placed after the verb in the classical/epic word order.
segments.s706-g106: عنان is the noun 'reins/bridle' and باره here qualifies it (sense of 'bearing/loaded'); تیزتگ را is the recipient phrase with را marking the definite person who receives them; سپرد is the past tense 'entrusted/handed over'. The whole segment means 'he handed the reins over to Tiztag.'
segments.s706-g107: ز زین = 'from the saddle' (ز is the poetic 'from' and زین is 'saddle'); برگرفتش is past 'he took up/removed it' (برگرفت + enclitic ش = 'it'); به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the curve (خم) of the lasso (کمند)'. The clause describes moving something from the saddle into the loop of the snare.
segments.s706-g108: بیاورد is past 'he brought (it)'; و here links actions 'and/then'; کردش is 'he made/did it' with the enclitic ش = 'it'; هم آنگه 'also/just so, then' is adverbial; ببند is an imperative/command 'bind/fasten' — in narrative this is reported command/instruction, so ببند appears as the quoted or implied order to bind what was arranged.
segments.s706-g109: همی‌خواست is a past-inchoative/intent form 'he was about to / intended to'; نستود (infinitival/past stem of ستودن) here functions as the verb 'to praise' following خواست; the following element (زو — written here as ز + و) refers to 'him' (archaic/object pronoun), and زینهار is an interjection 'beware/take heed' used as a warning instead of praise. The clause thus says he intended to praise but issued a warning.
segments.s706-g110: همی‌گفت is the imperfective/past-continuous 'he kept saying' or 'he repeatedly said'; کای is a vocative particle 'O'; نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying شهریار 'king' — so he keeps addressing the addressee as 'O renowned king.'
segments.s706-g111: چرا is the interrogative 'why'; ریخت here is used as the noun/act 'shedding/pouring' (bloodshed) rather than 'form'; خواهی is 2nd-person modal 'you wish / you will' (addressing the addressee); همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just'); خون من = 'my blood.' Together: 'Why do you wish to shed my blood?'
segments.s706-g112: ببخشای is an imperative/jussive 'forgive/spare' used as a plea; بر here means 'on/account of' or 'because of'; بخت وارون is a compound 'reversed/inverted fortune' = 'ill fate' or 'bad luck'; من is the possessor 'my.' The whole phrase is an appeal: 'forgive/spare me because of my ill fortune.'
segments.s706-g33: This clause lists subject then object then verb: یلان (subject, plural) + سینه (object, 'breasts' or 'bosoms') + برسم ... داد. برسم is an archaic/poetic form of رسیدن/رسیدن (here sense 'to reach/attain'), used in the sequence 'سینه برسم به...' to mean 'offered/held out their breasts to...'; داد is the past tense verb 'gave/offered'. Together the phrase means 'the heroes gave/offered their breasts to Bahram.'
segments.s706-g34: نیامد is past negative 'did not come' (here 'did not arise/occur'); همی is an adverb emphasizing continuative/habitual meaning ('ever / at all'); در غم is a locative phrase 'into/within sorrow'; از واژ یاد is a genitival phrase 'the memory of the overturn' (از = 'of/from', واژ used as a noun 'the overturn', یاد = 'memory'). Together: 'the memory of the overturn did not (ever) come into sorrow.'
segments.s706-g35: گرفتند is past plural 'they seized/took'. واژ here is an adjective/noun 'overturned' (the thing that was overturned) serving as the object. و simply links actions 'and'. بخوردند is past plural 'they ate'. نان is the direct object 'bread'. Word order is subject (implied) + verb + object; the two verbs are coordinated: 'they seized the overturned [thing] and ate bread.'
segments.s706-g36: This clause is inverted: نظاره (a noun 'view/observation') functions as 'looking' and stands before the locative phrase. ب + دان here combine as an older/poetic form of بدان = 'at that' or 'to that' (ب = 'to/at', دان part of the fused particle), followed by نامداران زنان 'the renowned women' (subject). The sense: 'the renowned women looked on at that.'
segments.s706-g37: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کشکین is a descriptive noun/adjective for a food item (here 'kashk‑based' or 'made of kashk'); بخوردند is past plural 'they ate'; می‌خواستند is past continuous/plural 'they wanted/were wishing'. The clause means 'when they ate the kashkīn, they wanted (i.e., longed for more / desired).'
segments.s706-g38: زبانها is plural 'tongues' (literal or figurative for 'speech'); به here marks instrument/association 'with' or 'by'; زمزم refers to the sacred/pure water Zamzam; بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/embellished'. Together: 'they adorned their tongues with Zamzam' — i.e. they sweetened/formed their speech by Zamzam (figuratively: they polished their words).
segments.s706-g39: زن پیر گفت = 'the old woman said'. ار is the conditional 'if'. میت is used here as 'death/the dead' and آرزوست is a nominal predicate 'is a wish / is what is desired'. The whole reported clause is 'If death is the desire...' (the old woman begins to name candidates after this conditional).
segments.s706-g40: میست is a proper name here (a woman called Meist); و 'and' links items; یکی نیز 'one also' or 'another one' identifies an additional person; کهنه is an adjective 'old' modifying the following name; کدوست is another proper name. The sequence lists two names: 'Meist, and another, the old Kaduost.'
segments.s706-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از تشنگی ('from thirst') gives the cause; تشنگی is a noun in the genitive relation introduced by از. خشک شدشان is the past verb شد plus the enclitic pronoun شان ('their'), so literally 'became dry for them'; دهن ('mouth') is the affected noun. Word order is inverted from modern colloquial (literal: 'when from thirst dry became to them mouth' → 'when their mouths became dry from thirst').
segments.s706-g26: بیامد is a past-tense form meaning 'came' (poetic/archaic variant of آمد). به خان is a prepositional phrase 'to the khan' (here 'khan' = inn/caravanserai), and یکی پیرزن is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain old woman' (یکی before the noun is the classical way to mean 'a certain/one'). The clause is a simple past action: 'an old woman came to the khan.'
segments.s706-g27: زبان را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the tongue' as the object. به چربی here expresses the means/instrument ('with/using fat'); بیاراستند is the past plural of بی‌آراستن 'to adorn/anoint', so the clause means 'they anointed (their) tongue with fat.' The verb is past and agrees with an implied plural subject.
segments.s706-g28: وزان is a fused classical form meaning 'and from that' (وز = و + زان/از آن), linking to the previously mentioned person; پیرزن is 'old woman'. آب و نان are the objects 'water and bread', and خواستند is past plural 'they asked/wanted'. The clause order places the source (from that old woman) before the objects and then the past verb 'asked.'
segments.s706-g29: زن پیر = 'the old woman' (noun + adjective). گفتار ایشان = 'their speech/words' (گفتار as direct object; ایشان = polite 'they/their'). شنید is past tense 'she heard'. The clause is straightforward S (subject) + object phrase + past verb typical of Persian.
segments.s706-g30: یکی کهنه غربیل = 'an old/worn sieve' with یکی = 'a certain' (indefinite). پیش آورید combines پیش ('forward/in front') with آورید, here used as an imperative/plural command 'bring (forward)' (آورید = bring; in epic style this form can be an instruction to attendants). So the clause functions as an imperative: 'Bring forward an old sieve.'
segments.s706-g31: برو is the imperative 'go'. بر here functions as a motion/prepositional particle 'onto/at', and به گسترده ('to the spread-out [cloth/pile]') indicates the place they should go to; یک پاره مشک is the object 'a piece/strip of musk'. The sequence is an imperative motion instruction: 'Go to the spread-out (thing) and take a piece of musk.'
segments.s706-g32: نهاده is the past participle 'placed/put' used here in an instructional/performative sequence meaning essentially 'placing/put it'. به غربیل uses به with the sense 'into/towards' to mark the container (the sieve). بر نان کشک = 'on the kashk-bread' (بر = on/upon, نان = bread, کشک = kashk/dried yogurt used as topping). Altogether: '(having) place it into the sieve over the kashk-bread' — an instruction on how to apply the musk.
segments.s706-g89: شنیدم is a first‑person past (I heard). که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'). چون here functions temporally as 'when/as'. ما is the subject 'we'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پرده is 'curtain' and سرای here names the dwelling/room (classical word‑order: modifier after noun); together ز پرده سرای = 'from behind the chamber's curtain'.
segments.s706-g90: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' (here modifying the action). چیدن رای is a verbal phrase where چیدن (to choose/arrange) takes رای ('counsel, plan') as its object; کردیم is the past-tense first‑person plural 'we did/made' and completes the clause: 'we formed/made many counsels/plans'. Word order is object after verb in classical style (چیدن راه کردیم رای = we chose many ways/plans).
segments.s706-g91: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'. بگزید is the simple past 'he chose/selected'. نستود is the past of ستودن 'he praised' used here in close sequence (classical narrative often strings actions), and را is the definite object marker attached to what follows or to the implied object; overall this clause reports the sovereign's actions ('the sovereign chose and praised').
segments.s706-g92: جهان جوی is a compound: جهان 'world' with جوی (from جویا/جوی 'seeker, search') = 'world-seeker' or 'one who seeks the world'. بی‌تار و بی‌پود is a paired adjectival phrase 'unstrung and without weft' (literal weaving metaphors). را again marks the (definite) object/topic that the sovereign praised/selected: 'the world‑seeker, unstrung and without weft.'
segments.s706-g93: This nominal phrase gives a number of cavalry: 'سه هزار از سواران مرد' = 'three thousand of the horsemen'. ابا here is irregular in the supplied senses and seems to function as a particle/introductory element in the poem (or a scribal/metrical form); از introduces the partitive 'of' and سواران مرد = 'horsemen, men'.
segments.s706-g94: This is an interrogative clause. کجا 'where' is the question word; پای دارند literally 'have a foot' = 'have a footing/stand' (پای here is 'foot/foothold'); دارند is the present/plural auxiliary 'they have'. روز نبرد is a genitive phrase 'the day of battle' (noun + modifier). Together: 'Where will they have a footing on the day of battle?'
segments.s706-g95: بدان is a classical locution equal to به + آن 'to/at that (place or matter)' (here 'there/at that'), followed by تا + بیاید: تا introduces a temporal/subordinating clause 'until' and بیاید is the subjunctive/present form 'he may come / he comes' used for future/expected arrival. پس 'then' and ما 'we' introduce دمان: in context دمان is an archaic/formal verb/narrative form meaning 'we remained/waited' (the printed senses listed are nominal, so the verbal sense is irregular/archaic). Overall: 'We remained there until he came.'
segments.s706-g96: چو = 'when'. بینم is the first‑person (subjunctive/optative) form 'I see' used in narrative sequence to mean 'when I see/meet'. مر is the dative clitic 'to me' (used idiomatically here with perception/encounter), او را is the accusative 'him' with the object marker را (object is doubled for emphasis/clarity), and سرآرم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will lift/place my head' (سرآوردن/سرآرم used figuratively 'to show oneself / to raise one's head'). زمان 'time' here is adverbial/temporal 'at that time' or 'then'. The clause means 'when I see him, I will then raise my head (i.e. make myself seen) at the appointed time.'

sentences.s706-s49: They drew tight the reins of all the horses; they drew the whole host around that grove.
sentences.s706-s50: The light horsemen drew (their weapons) tight; they seized the Indian sword in hand.
sentences.s706-s51: They set the whole reed-bed on fire; they struck the host one by one, throwing it into disorder.
sentences.s706-s52: The reed-bed became wholly ablaze; some were slain and others burned.
sentences.s706-s41: They said to Bahram, “Anushah‑Badi — why did you come by the reed‑bed road?”
sentences.s706-s42: For there stood a mighty army before the inner court, all fighters with their hands washed in blood.
sentences.s706-s43: Thus Bahram said, 'Let there be no rider except from the king's host.'
sentences.s706-s44: They descended into that reed‑bed and there enclosed the whole fighting in a tight space between them.
sentences.s706-s29: Bahrām said to her, “O chaste woman, tell me this tale.”
sentences.s706-s30: For this was from wisdom on Bahram's part; and if he had chosen desire from passion...
sentences.s706-s31: The old woman said to him, “O famed man, why has a demon darkened your eye?”
sentences.s706-s32: Do you not know that Bahram, son of Gushasp, — Chuba, son of Hormoz, roused a steed?
sentences.s706-s21: I cut the gourd — its top was new — I made a cup and set it on it.
sentences.s706-s22: Bahram said to her, 'How could it be? Which cups could be better than those?'
sentences.s706-s23: The old woman went and brought a cup; from that cup Bahram became joyful.
sentences.s706-s24: Someone set a full cup upon his shoe, so that the old woman too would become happy.
sentences.s706-s61: When he heard (it), he praised, kissed the ground, and repeatedly cried “afarin” (bravo).
sentences.s706-s62: From that grove Bahram became Tabari; with him brave, fortunate followers accompanied (him).
sentences.s706-s63: He was and became at ease, and from there departed; he strode proudly toward the khaghan (text has a dubious final item).
sentences.s706-s37: When the sun revealed its secret on the heavens, the war-commander struck the drum again.
sentences.s706-s38: He mustered as many troops as he had; the illustrious men took up the road.
sentences.s706-s39: On a certain reed-bed there was a new lamb; many of the people there were reed-cutters.
sentences.s706-s40: When from afar they saw Bahram — and such a famished, self-willed host —
sentences.s706-s1: When the bright sun arrayed the hour, the vanguard came up close to the king.
sentences.s706-s2: No one was seen inside the curtained inner chamber; likewise, that very tent at its foot was not seen by anyone.
sentences.s706-s3: The vanguard came and told this to the king; the king's heart grew anxious because of that eager warrior.
sentences.s706-s4: He chose from those fighters three thousand armored and stout‑limbed horsemen.
sentences.s706-s9: Goshasp — the heroic breast and neck — rode a steed from one flank of the host.
sentences.s706-s10: They drove the army onto a wrong path and were reciting the words of kings.
sentences.s706-s11: From afar there appeared a single torn hamlet; there was no great man at its gate.
sentences.s706-s12: Bahram was riding before the inner court, remorseful, his heart full of pain and blood.
sentences.s706-s5: He told (Nastud) to mount; he checked the champions' charge in the midst of them.
sentences.s706-s6: Nastud rode on, his heart full of pain; there was no man like Bahram on the day of battle.
sentences.s706-s7: Likewise that same Bahram with his army was not safe on the roads nor in his realm.
sentences.s706-s8: He rode off the road, his heart full of fear; he was carrying gold and silver with him.
sentences.s706-s25: He said to her, "O mother, you learned one, what knowledge have you of the affairs of the world?"
sentences.s706-s26: The old woman said to him, "I have heard so much talk that my brain has grown old because of it."
sentences.s706-s27: Today many people came from the city; they say it is merely a fight with clubs.
sentences.s706-s28: When his army came near the king, the spahbed fled and was left without troops.
sentences.s706-s33: Let him laugh and go—whoever has sense; no one will count him among the neck-throwers.
sentences.s706-s34: Bahrām‑ger said to him, 'If you desire that, do thus: make him drink in a gourd.'
sentences.s706-s35: In this manner place a sieve upon the barley‑bread and keep it before (it) until the barley is reaped.
sentences.s706-s36: Thereupon they too enjoyed one calm night with the stew; he sought the desire of his heart and found himself unsuccessful.
sentences.s706-s57: Do not pull me away; let me run up to you so that I may come to your wretched, poor dwelling.
sentences.s706-s58: Bahram said to him, 'I am a man like you; I do not want there to be fighting on the plain.'
sentences.s706-s59: I will not take your head — shame would befall me — that I, like you, should come to the war as a rider.
sentences.s706-s60: If you find release from my hand, go and seek [your fortune]; tell Khosrow whatever you saw of me.
sentences.s706-s53: When Bahram Gord saw Chonestud, he entrusted the reins to Tiztag.
sentences.s706-s54: He took it up from the saddle, brought it into the curve of the lasso, and then said, 'Bind it likewise.'

sentences.s706-s56: Why would you shed my blood? Have mercy on me for my ill-fated lot.
sentences.s706-s17: The heroes offered their breasts to Bahram; the remembrance of the overturn never came into sorrow.
sentences.s706-s18: They seized the overturned thing and ate bread; the renowned women looked on.
sentences.s706-s19: When they ate the kashkīn, they wished for more; they adorned their tongues with Zamzam.
sentences.s706-s20: The old woman said, 'If death is your desire, (then) Meist — and another, the old Kaduost.'
sentences.s706-s13: When their mouths grew dry from thirst, an old woman came to the khan (inn).
sentences.s706-s14: They anointed the tongue with fat, and from that old woman they asked for water and bread.
sentences.s706-s15: The old woman heard their speech; 'Bring forward an old, worn sieve,' (she said).
sentences.s706-s16: Go to the spread-out cloth and take a piece of musk, placing it in the sieve over the kashk-bread.

sentences.s706-s46: The sovereign chose and praised the world-seeker, the one unstrung and without weft.
sentences.s706-s47: There were three thousand men of the horsemen — where will they stand on the day of battle?
sentences.s706-s48: We stayed there until he came; when I see him I will lift up my head at that time.
